FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. ABCD Study at Year 2 (2018-2020) with N = 10,258/11,875 participants from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis using Modified Poisson regression to examine associations between cyberbullying and eating disorder symptoms.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in International Journal of Eating Disorders (2023).

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures cyberbullying (defined as aggressive intentional act via electronic contact) as the independent variable. Cyberbullying is not a measure of smartphone or social media use. Per criteria: "Cyberbullying alone (without a separately-measured smartphone or SM variable) does NOT qualify." No separately-measured phone/SM variable paired with mental health outcomes.

notes: Fails on FT-IC2 because cyberbullying is measured as IV, not smartphone/social media use. The construct does not isolate phone or SM activity.

FT_IC1.evidence: Title and abstract explicitly reference U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; meets default rule for MET on FT-IC1 even without PDF.

FT_IC5.evidence: Abstract indicates original empirical analysis: "We used mixed-effects models to analyze associations between baseline self-reported screen time and parent-"

FT_IC2.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract mentions "self-reported screen time" but does not clarify whether construct isolates phone/SM activity or is a multi-device/multi-activity composite. Cannot determine if it meets FT-IC2 requirements without PDF.

notes: FT-IC2 is UNKNOWN due to no PDF and insufficient abstract detail on whether screen time isolates phone/SM or bundles TV/console/phone. Requires PDF retrieval to determine eligibility.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort study—the Dutch ABCD, NOT the U.S. ABCD Study. Per FT-IC1 NOT_MET rule: "Different cohort sharing the 'ABCD' acronym — including Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (Dutch ABCD)". Methods section confirms this is the Dutch cohort, fifth and sixth waves, with 2,663 Dutch adolescents from Amsterdam.

notes: Excluded at FT-IC1. Title and abstract use the acronym ABCD but this is the Dutch Amsterdam Born Children and their Development cohort, not the U.S. ABCD Study. Requires verification of cohort identity by reading methods section, which confirms Dutch sample. Exclusion code: FT-EC1.
